name: Latency SLO Guard
description: Monitors service response time against a defined SLO target. Triggers an alert or blocks deployment when the p99 latency exceeds the threshold. Ideal for user-facing APIs where response time directly impacts experience.

nodes.label: Latency SLO Guard
nodes.description: Ensures p99 response time stays within the defined SLO target. Measures end-to-end latency from client request to response.

name: Coupling Guard
description: Measures component fan-out (number of outgoing dependencies) and flags modules that exceed the threshold. High fan-out indicates tight coupling and fragile change propagation.

nodes.label: Coupling Guard
nodes.description: Checks that no component has more than N outgoing dependencies. Prevents tightly-coupled designs that resist change.

name: Deployment Frequency
description: Tracks how often the team deploys to production. One of the four DORA metrics. Low deployment frequency often correlates with large, risky batch releases.

nodes.label: Deployment Frequency
nodes.description: Measures deployments per week/month. Target: on-demand or at least weekly for high-performing teams.

name: Security Dependency Scan
description: Scans all runtime and build dependencies for known CVEs before deployment. Blocks releases containing critical or high-severity vulnerabilities. Essential for supply-chain security.

nodes.label: Security Dependency Scan
nodes.description: Scans dependencies for CVEs. Fails pipeline on critical/high severity findings. Uses tools like Snyk, Trivy or npm audit.

name: API Contract Compliance
description: Validates that API implementations conform to their OpenAPI/AsyncAPI specifications. Catches breaking changes before they reach consumers. Critical for microservice architectures.

nodes.label: API Contract Compliance
nodes.description: Validates implementation against OpenAPI/AsyncAPI spec. Detects breaking changes (removed fields, changed types, dropped endpoints).

name: Data Residency Check
description: Continuously verifies that personal data is stored and processed only in approved geographic regions. Required for GDPR, data sovereignty laws, and contractual obligations.

nodes.label: Data Residency Check
nodes.description: Verifies that PII is stored only in approved regions (e.g. EU-only for GDPR). Checks cloud resource tags, database locations, and CDN configs.

name: Error Budget Burn Rate
description: Tracks the consumption rate of the error budget (derived from SLO). When the burn rate exceeds thresholds, it triggers alerts or freezes feature releases until reliability improves. Key SRE practice.

nodes.label: Error Budget Burn Rate
nodes.description: Monitors error budget consumption. Alert at {{ALERT_THRESHOLD_PCT}}% consumed in first quarter of window. Feature freeze at {{FREEZE_THRESHOLD_PCT}}% consumed.

name: Test Coverage Gate
description: Enforces a minimum code coverage percentage on every PR/deployment. Prevents untested code from reaching production. Best combined with mutation testing for quality, not just quantity.

nodes.label: Test Coverage Gate
nodes.description: Blocks deployment when line/branch coverage falls below the threshold. Ratchets up: new code must meet or exceed current coverage.
nodes.threshold: ≥ {{MIN_COVERAGE_PCT}}% line coverage, no decrease on diff

name: Lead Time for Changes
description: Measures the time from code commit to running in production. One of the four DORA metrics. Shorter lead times indicate a healthier CI/CD pipeline and faster feedback loops.

nodes.label: Lead Time for Changes
nodes.description: Tracks time from commit to production deploy. Target: less than one day for elite performers (DORA).

name: Mean Time to Recovery
description: Measures how long it takes to restore service after a production incident. One of the four DORA metrics. Shorter MTTR indicates better incident response and system resilience.

nodes.label: Mean Time to Recovery
nodes.description: Tracks time from incident detection to service restoration. Target: less than one hour for elite performers (DORA).

name: System Availability
description: Ensures the system meets its availability target. Ubiquitous requirement — applies at all times without preconditions.

nodes.label: System Availability
nodes.action: be available {{AVAILABILITY_TARGET}} of the time, measured monthly
nodes.rationale: Business SLA requires three-nines availability to meet customer contracts and avoid penalty clauses.

name: Auto-Scaling Under Load
description: Event-driven requirement that triggers horizontal scaling when load exceeds a threshold.

nodes.label: Auto-Scaling Under Load
nodes.trigger: incoming request rate exceeds {{SCALE_TRIGGER_RPS}} requests per second
nodes.action: automatically scale out compute instances within {{SCALE_OUT_SECONDS}} seconds to maintain response time below 500ms
nodes.rationale: Traffic spikes during promotions and seasonal events must be handled without manual intervention.

name: API Authentication
description: All API requests must be authenticated. Ubiquitous security requirement with no preconditions.

nodes.label: API Authentication
nodes.action: authenticate all API requests using {{AUTH_PROTOCOL}} before processing
nodes.rationale: Unauthenticated access exposes sensitive data and operations to unauthorized parties.

name: Response Time Under Load
description: State-driven requirement: while the system is under normal operating conditions, API responses must complete within a target time.

nodes.label: Response Time Under Load
nodes.precondition: the system is operating under normal load (< 80% CPU, < 70% memory)
nodes.action: respond to any API request within {{RESPONSE_TIME_MS}}ms at the {{PERCENTILE}} percentile
nodes.rationale: Perceived responsiveness directly impacts user satisfaction and conversion rates.

name: Data Retention Policy
description: Unwanted-behaviour requirement: prevents unbounded data growth by archiving old data.

nodes.label: Data Retention Policy
nodes.unwanted_condition: transactional data is older than {{RETENTION_DAYS}} days
nodes.action: archive the data to cold storage and remove it from the primary database
nodes.rationale: Unbounded data growth degrades query performance and increases storage costs.

name: GDPR Data Encryption
description: Optional feature requirement: when handling EU user data, encryption at rest is mandatory.

nodes.label: GDPR Data Encryption
nodes.feature: EU user personal data is stored or processed
nodes.action: encrypt all personal data at rest using {{ENCRYPTION_ALGORITHM}} and in transit using {{MIN_TLS_VERSION}}+
nodes.rationale: GDPR Article 32 requires appropriate technical measures to protect personal data.

name: Disaster Recovery Failover
description: Event-driven requirement: when the primary region fails, the system must failover to a secondary region within a defined time window.

nodes.label: Disaster Recovery Failover
nodes.trigger: the primary region becomes unavailable for more than {{OUTAGE_THRESHOLD_SECONDS}} seconds
nodes.action: automatically failover to the secondary region with an RTO of {{RTO_MINUTES}} minutes and RPO of {{RPO_MINUTES}} minute
nodes.rationale: Business continuity requires rapid recovery from regional outages to minimize revenue loss.

name: Audit Logging
description: Ubiquitous requirement: all state-changing operations must be logged for audit and compliance purposes.

nodes.label: Audit Logging
nodes.action: log all state-changing operations with timestamp, actor identity, action type, affected resource, and before/after values
nodes.rationale: Regulatory compliance (SOX, PCI-DSS) and security forensics require a complete, immutable audit trail.

name: API Rate Limiting
description: Complex requirement combining state and event conditions to protect the system from abuse.

nodes.label: API Rate Limiting
nodes.precondition: the API gateway is operating normally
nodes.trigger: a single client exceeds {{RATE_LIMIT_RPM}} requests per minute
nodes.action: reject excess requests with HTTP 429 and include a Retry-After header
nodes.rationale: Prevents individual clients from monopolizing resources and degrading service for others.

name: WCAG Accessibility
description: Ubiquitous requirement ensuring the UI meets accessibility standards for all users.

nodes.label: WCAG Accessibility
nodes.action: conform to WCAG {{WCAG_VERSION}} Level {{WCAG_LEVEL}} for all user-facing pages and interactive components
nodes.rationale: Inclusive design ensures the product is usable by people with disabilities and meets legal requirements in many jurisdictions.

name: Page Load Time
description: Performance: state-driven requirement ensuring web pages load within acceptable time under normal operating conditions.

nodes.label: Page Load Time
nodes.precondition: the user is on a standard broadband connection and the system is under normal load
nodes.action: render the initial page and make it interactive (TTI) within {{TTI_SECONDS}} seconds for 95% of users
nodes.rationale: Studies show that pages taking longer than 3 seconds lose ~53% of mobile visitors. Core Web Vitals impact SEO ranking.

name: System Throughput
description: Performance: ubiquitous requirement defining the minimum transaction processing rate the system must sustain.

nodes.label: System Throughput
nodes.action: sustain a minimum of {{MIN_TPS}} transactions per second under peak load without degrading response times beyond defined SLOs
nodes.rationale: Peak traffic during promotional events requires sufficient throughput headroom to avoid revenue-impacting degradation.

name: CPU Utilisation Ceiling
description: Performance: state-driven requirement preventing runaway resource usage under normal operating conditions.

nodes.label: CPU Utilisation Ceiling
nodes.precondition: the system is handling normal production traffic
nodes.action: keep average CPU utilisation below {{CPU_CEILING_PCT}}% on all compute nodes, triggering auto-scaling before the ceiling is reached
nodes.rationale: Sustained CPU above 80% leaves no headroom for traffic spikes and can cascade into latency degradation.

name: User Onboarding Time
description: Usability: ubiquitous requirement ensuring first-time users can reach core value quickly without external help.

nodes.label: User Onboarding Time
nodes.action: enable a first-time user to complete the primary onboarding flow and reach the core value moment within {{ONBOARDING_MINUTES}} minutes without external assistance
nodes.rationale: Time-to-value is a key driver of trial-to-paid conversion. Friction in onboarding directly impacts revenue.

name: Error Recovery Guidance
description: Usability: ubiquitous requirement ensuring every system error provides actionable recovery guidance.

nodes.label: Error Recovery Guidance
nodes.action: display a human-readable error message with a specific corrective action or next step for every user-facing error state
nodes.rationale: Dead-end error states cause abandonment. Users who understand what went wrong and how to fix it have significantly higher task completion rates.

name: Automated Test Coverage
description: Maintainability: ubiquitous requirement ensuring sufficient automated test coverage of business logic.

nodes.label: Automated Test Coverage
nodes.action: maintain automated unit and integration test coverage of at least {{MIN_COVERAGE_PCT}}% for all business-logic modules, enforced via CI pipeline gates
nodes.rationale: Test coverage acts as a safety net for refactoring. Below 80% coverage, defect escape rates increase significantly.

name: Code Complexity Limit
description: Maintainability: ubiquitous requirement capping cyclomatic complexity per function to keep code understandable and testable.

nodes.label: Code Complexity Limit
nodes.action: keep cyclomatic complexity per function below {{MAX_COMPLEXITY}}, enforced by static analysis in the CI pipeline with build failure on violation
nodes.rationale: Functions with complexity above 10 require exponentially more test cases and are significantly harder to debug and refactor.

name: CI Pipeline Duration
description: Maintainability: state-driven requirement keeping the CI/CD feedback loop short enough to maintain developer flow.

nodes.label: CI Pipeline Duration
nodes.precondition: a developer pushes a commit to a feature branch
nodes.action: complete the full build, lint, test, and security scan pipeline within {{MAX_PIPELINE_MINUTES}} minutes
nodes.rationale: Pipelines longer than 10 minutes break developer flow, discourage frequent commits, and slow down feedback on regressions.

name: REST API Standard
description: Interoperability: ubiquitous requirement mandating OpenAPI documentation for all public service endpoints.

nodes.label: REST API Standard
nodes.action: expose all public service interfaces as RESTful APIs documented with OpenAPI 3.0 specifications, version-controlled alongside the service code
nodes.rationale: OpenAPI documentation enables automated client generation, contract testing, and accelerates third-party integrations.

name: API Backward Compatibility
description: Interoperability: unwanted-behaviour requirement preventing breaking changes from being silently introduced into published APIs.

nodes.label: API Backward Compatibility
nodes.unwanted_condition: a breaking change is introduced to a published API (field removal, type change, or status code change)
nodes.action: increment the major version and maintain the previous version for a minimum of {{DEPRECATION_MONTHS}} months with a deprecation notice
nodes.rationale: Unannounced breaking changes break partner integrations. Stable contracts are a prerequisite for ecosystem adoption.

name: Multi-Factor Authentication
description: Security: optional requirement mandating MFA for privileged access and sensitive operations.

nodes.label: Multi-Factor Authentication
nodes.feature: a user accesses administrative functions or performs a high-risk operation
nodes.action: require multi-factor authentication using TOTP or a hardware key before granting access
nodes.rationale: Stolen credentials alone are insufficient to compromise admin accounts. MFA prevents the majority of account-takeover attacks.

name: Encryption in Transit
description: Security: ubiquitous requirement mandating TLS for all network communication carrying sensitive data.

nodes.label: Encryption in Transit
nodes.action: encrypt all network communication carrying personal data or credentials using {{MIN_TLS_VERSION}} or higher, rejecting plaintext connections
nodes.rationale: Plaintext transport exposes data to interception. TLS is the baseline for protecting data in motion per GDPR, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA.

name: Dependency Vulnerability Scanning
description: Security: event-driven requirement blocking deployments that include known critical vulnerabilities in dependencies.

nodes.label: Dependency Vulnerability Scanning
nodes.trigger: a new build is triggered or a critical CVE is published for a dependency in use
nodes.action: scan all runtime dependencies for known CVEs and block the pipeline on any critical (CVSS ≥ {{CRITICAL_CVSS}}) or high (CVSS ≥ {{HIGH_CVSS}}) severity finding
nodes.rationale: Supply-chain attacks via compromised dependencies are a leading attack vector. Log4Shell showed that a single library flaw can expose millions of systems.

name: Service Health Checks
description: Manageability: ubiquitous requirement ensuring every service exposes a standardised health endpoint for monitoring and orchestration.

nodes.label: Service Health Checks
nodes.action: expose a /health endpoint returning HTTP 200 with liveness and readiness status, including dependency health, within {{HEALTH_RESPONSE_MS}}ms
nodes.rationale: Orchestrators and load balancers require health endpoints to route traffic away from unhealthy instances. Missing checks mean zombie processes receive live traffic.

name: Centralised Structured Logging
description: Manageability: ubiquitous requirement mandating structured, centralised log collection across all services.

nodes.label: Centralised Structured Logging
nodes.action: emit all logs in structured JSON format with correlation IDs, severity, timestamp, and service name, aggregated into a centralised log store with a minimum {{LOG_RETENTION_DAYS}}-day retention
nodes.rationale: Unstructured logs scattered across hosts are unqueryable in production incidents. Structured, centralised logs reduce mean time to diagnosis.

name: SLO Breach Alerting
description: Manageability: event-driven requirement triggering on-call alerting before an SLO breach occurs.

nodes.label: SLO Breach Alerting
nodes.trigger: any service metric (error rate, latency, availability) approaches {{BUDGET_ALERT_PCT}}% of its SLO error budget
nodes.action: fire a P2 alert to the on-call engineer via PagerDuty with the affected metric, current value, and a link to the relevant dashboard
nodes.rationale: Reacting to SLO breaches after the fact is too late. Burn-rate alerting allows intervention before users are impacted.

name: Idempotent Operations
description: Dependability: ubiquitous requirement ensuring that retried or duplicated requests do not cause unintended side effects.

nodes.label: Idempotent Operations
nodes.action: implement idempotency keys for all state-changing API operations, ensuring that duplicate requests within a {{IDEMPOTENCY_WINDOW_HOURS}}-hour window produce the same result without creating duplicate records
nodes.rationale: Network retries are unavoidable. Without idempotency, transient failures cause double-charges, duplicate orders, or data corruption.

name: Referential Data Integrity
description: Dependability: ubiquitous requirement preventing orphaned or inconsistent data across service boundaries.

nodes.label: Referential Data Integrity
nodes.action: enforce referential integrity for all cross-entity relationships at the database or application layer, rejecting any operation that would leave orphaned records
nodes.rationale: Orphaned records cause silent calculation errors, billing anomalies, and compliance violations that are expensive to detect and remediate.

name: Graceful Degradation
description: Availability: event-driven requirement ensuring the system remains partially functional when a non-critical dependency is unavailable.

nodes.label: Graceful Degradation
nodes.trigger: a non-critical downstream service or third-party dependency becomes unavailable
nodes.action: continue serving core functionality using cached data or a reduced feature set, displaying a clear status notice to affected users rather than returning an error
nodes.rationale: Total failures caused by partial outages erode user trust far more than reduced functionality. Circuit breakers and fallbacks are the standard mitigation.

name: Infrastructure as Code
description: Automatability: ubiquitous requirement mandating all infrastructure be defined and provisioned via code.

nodes.label: Infrastructure as Code
nodes.action: define and provision all cloud infrastructure using IaC tooling (Terraform, Pulumi, or CloudFormation), with all changes applied exclusively via version-controlled pipelines
nodes.rationale: Manual infrastructure changes are untracked, unrepeatable, and create snowflake environments. IaC enables reproducible environments and disaster recovery.

name: Zero-Downtime Deployment
description: Automatability: ubiquitous requirement ensuring deployments do not interrupt active users.

nodes.label: Zero-Downtime Deployment
nodes.action: deploy all service updates using blue/green or rolling deployment strategies with automated smoke tests, completing without dropping any in-flight requests
nodes.rationale: Maintenance windows are incompatible with global 24/7 services. Zero-downtime deploys allow continuous delivery without SLA violations.

name: API Response Time
description: Performance: REST API endpoints must respond within 200ms for the majority of requests under normal load.

nodes.label: API Response Time
nodes.precondition: the system is handling normal production traffic
nodes.action: respond to REST API requests within {{P90_RESPONSE_MS}}ms at the 90th percentile, with a hard ceiling of {{P99_CEILING_MS}}ms at the 99th percentile
nodes.rationale: API latency directly impacts downstream integration performance. Amazon found every 100ms of latency cost 1% in sales. Sub-200ms responses are required for a seamless UX.

name: Authentication Response Time
description: Performance: user login and token verification must complete within 2 seconds to avoid abandonment.

nodes.label: Authentication Response Time
nodes.precondition: the system is under normal load with up to {{MAX_CONCURRENT_LOGINS}} concurrent login attempts
nodes.action: complete user authentication (credential validation, token issuance) within {{AUTH_RESPONSE_SECONDS}} seconds for {{AUTH_PERCENTILE}} of requests
nodes.rationale: Login delays over 2 seconds erode user trust and increase abandonment. The authentication step is the first impression of system reliability.

name: Database Query Response Time
description: Performance: OLTP database queries must execute within 100ms to prevent query latency from dominating API response times.

nodes.label: Database Query Response Time
nodes.precondition: the system is running standard OLTP operations on a production-size dataset
nodes.action: execute standard transactional queries within {{DB_P90_MS}}ms at the 90th percentile, monitored via continuous database performance telemetry
nodes.rationale: Database query time is typically the largest single contributor to API latency. Queries exceeding 100ms expose APIs to SLO breaches under concurrent load.

name: Search Functionality Response Time
description: Performance: search queries must return results within 500ms to maintain a usable search experience.

nodes.label: Search Functionality Response Time
nodes.precondition: a user submits a search query against a production-size dataset
nodes.action: return search results within {{SEARCH_P90_MS}}ms for 90% of queries, using appropriate indexing strategies validated by performance testing with representative data
nodes.rationale: Users expect near-instant search results. Results taking over 1 second cause a sharp drop in search engagement and perceived product quality.

name: Report Generation Time
description: Performance: standard business reports must complete within 30 seconds to avoid user abandonment of reporting workflows.

nodes.label: Report Generation Time
nodes.precondition: a user requests a standard report against a production-size dataset
nodes.action: complete standard report generation and delivery within {{REPORT_MAX_SECONDS}} seconds, validated by volume testing with production-scale data
nodes.rationale: Reports taking over 30 seconds are frequently abandoned. If generation is longer, an async model with notification should be used instead of a blocking request.

name: Primary Workflow Task Success Rate
description: Usability: users must be able to complete the primary workflow without external help, measured by task success rate in usability testing.

nodes.label: Primary Workflow Task Success Rate
nodes.action: achieve a task success rate of at least {{MIN_TASK_SUCCESS_PCT}}% for the primary user workflow in moderated usability testing with representative users, without external assistance
nodes.rationale: Task success rate is the most direct measure of usability. Airbnb doubled revenue by focusing on removing friction from primary workflows.

name: API Documentation Coverage
description: Maintainability: all public interfaces must be fully documented to enable safe integration and reduce support burden.

nodes.label: API Documentation Coverage
nodes.action: maintain 100% documentation coverage for all public API endpoints, including request/response schemas, error codes, and usage examples, kept current via peer-reviewed documentation standards
nodes.rationale: Undocumented APIs force integrators to reverse-engineer behaviour, increasing support tickets and integration errors. Complete docs reduce integration time and defect rates.

name: Mission-Critical Service SLA
description: Availability: mission-critical services require four-nines uptime (99.99%), achieved through multi-region active-active architecture.

nodes.label: Mission-Critical Service SLA
nodes.action: maintain {{SLA_TARGET}}% availability for mission-critical services, implemented via multi-region active-active architecture with automated failover
nodes.rationale: Four-nines uptime is the industry standard for revenue-generating and safety-critical systems. Each additional nine costs exponentially more but protects against high-impact outages.

name: Internal Tools SLA
description: Availability: internal and back-office tools have a lower but still contractual uptime target of 99.0%, backed by standard backup and restore.

nodes.label: Internal Tools SLA
nodes.action: maintain {{SLA_TARGET}}% availability for internal tooling and back-office systems, backed by standard backup-and-restore recovery procedures
nodes.rationale: Internal tools don't require the same investment as customer-facing systems, but unplanned downtime still disrupts employee productivity. A 99.0% target balances cost and impact.

name: Batch Processing SLA
description: Availability: batch and scheduled processing workloads require 95% availability with queue-based retry logic to handle transient failures.

nodes.label: Batch Processing SLA
nodes.action: achieve {{MIN_BATCH_SUCCESS_PCT}}% successful completion rate for scheduled batch jobs, using queue-based retry logic to tolerate transient failures without manual intervention
nodes.rationale: Batch jobs are more tolerant of brief delays than interactive systems. Queue-based retries absorb transient failures without operator intervention, making 95% a realistic and cost-effective target.

name: Async Messaging Standard
description: Interoperability: asynchronous event processing must use standardised messaging protocols to ensure reliable, decoupled integration across services.

nodes.label: Async Messaging Standard
nodes.action: implement all asynchronous inter-service communication using AMQP via {{MESSAGE_BROKER}} (or MQTT for IoT workloads), with end-to-end message tracing enabled
nodes.rationale: Standardised messaging protocols decouple producers from consumers, enable broker-level durability, and allow services to evolve independently without tight-coupling.

name: Data Format Standard
description: Interoperability: all service payloads must conform to a shared enterprise data schema to preserve semantic integrity across system boundaries.

nodes.label: Data Format Standard
nodes.action: define a canonical enterprise data schema (JSON-LD for semantic web contexts, Protobuf for high-throughput services) and validate all service payloads against it via semantic unit tests
nodes.rationale: Without a shared schema, a 'Customer ID' in one system has a different meaning in another. Semantic drift across services causes silent data corruption and expensive migration projects.

name: Principle of Least Privilege
description: Security: users and services must be granted only the minimum permissions required for their role, reducing blast radius from compromised accounts.

nodes.label: Principle of Least Privilege
nodes.action: grant every user account and service identity the minimum set of permissions necessary to perform its function, with access reviewed {{ACCESS_REVIEW_FREQUENCY}} and excess permissions revoked
nodes.rationale: Least privilege limits blast radius when credentials are compromised. Google's BeyondCorp and Zero Trust models prove that eliminating implicit trust is the most effective defence against lateral movement.

name: Configuration Propagation Speed
description: Manageability: global configuration changes must propagate to all nodes within 5 minutes to enable timely operational control.

nodes.label: Configuration Propagation Speed
nodes.precondition: an operator applies a configuration change via the control plane
nodes.action: propagate configuration changes to all running nodes within {{CONFIG_PROPAGATION_SECONDS}} seconds, with propagation lag monitored and alerted if the threshold is exceeded
nodes.rationale: Slow config propagation delays operational responses to incidents. Feature flag changes, rate limit updates, and circuit breaker thresholds must take effect before an incident escalates.

name: Alert Delivery Reliability
description: Manageability: critical operational alerts must be reliably delivered to the paging system to ensure on-call engineers are notified without delay.

nodes.label: Alert Delivery Reliability
nodes.trigger: a critical alert condition is detected by the monitoring system
nodes.action: deliver critical alerts to {{PAGING_SYSTEM}} within {{ALERT_DELIVERY_SECONDS}} seconds of detection with {{ALERT_RELIABILITY_PCT}}% delivery reliability, verified by periodic end-to-end alert drills
nodes.rationale: An alert that doesn't arrive is worse than no alert system at all. The Target breach worsened because alerts were ignored due to noise. High delivery reliability ensures the team actually gets notified.

name: API Scripting Coverage
description: Automatability: all core system functions must be accessible via API to enable deterministic automation without UI interaction.

nodes.label: API Scripting Coverage
nodes.action: expose 100% of core data-entry and state-changing operations via headless REST API, enabling complete UI-less interaction for scripting, RPA, and programmatic integration
nodes.rationale: Systems without complete API coverage force automation tools to use brittle UI scraping. API-first design is a prerequisite for both RPA and agentic AI workflows.

name: Intelligent Document Extraction Accuracy
description: Automatability: ML-powered document processing must achieve sufficient accuracy to be trusted for automated data extraction workflows.

nodes.label: Intelligent Document Extraction Accuracy
nodes.action: achieve an F1 score of at least {{MIN_F1_PCT}}% for ML-based extraction of structured fields from unstructured documents (PDFs, forms), validated against a representative labelled test corpus
nodes.rationale: Below 95% accuracy, the cost of human review corrections exceeds the automation savings. F1 score balances precision and recall to prevent both missed extractions and false positives.

name: AI Agent Autonomous Resolution Rate
description: Automatability: AI agents must autonomously resolve a high proportion of routine inquiries without human escalation to deliver meaningful automation ROI.

nodes.label: AI Agent Autonomous Resolution Rate
nodes.action: enable AI agents to autonomously resolve at least {{MIN_RESOLUTION_PCT}}% of routine customer or internal inquiries without human escalation, measured by success rate analytics over a rolling {{MEASUREMENT_WINDOW_DAYS}}-day window
nodes.rationale: Below 80% autonomous resolution, the cost of human escalation handling erodes the ROI of the agentic AI investment. Salesforce Agentforce targets this threshold as the commercial viability benchmark.

name: AI Agent Truthfulness
description: Dependability: AI agents must cite verifiable sources for every factual claim, preventing hallucinations that could cause legal liability or user harm.

nodes.label: AI Agent Truthfulness
nodes.action: require AI agents to cite a specific retrieved document or governed data source for every factual claim, with groundedness score validated via LLM-as-a-Judge and audited monthly
nodes.rationale: Air Canada was held legally liable when its chatbot hallucinated a policy. The NIST AI RMF requires explainability. RAG-grounded agents eliminate hallucinations by design.

name: Transaction Reliability
description: Dependability: the system must complete the overwhelming majority of transactions without error to be justifiably trusted for business-critical operations.

nodes.label: Transaction Reliability
nodes.action: complete at least {{RELIABILITY_TARGET}}% of all business transactions without system-induced errors, tracked via Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and error rate dashboards
nodes.rationale: Five-nines transaction reliability (1 failure per 100,000) is the threshold for systems trusted with financial, medical, or safety-critical operations. Below this, error rates cause measurable downstream harm.

name: Technology Stack Selection
description: Template ADR for selecting the primary technology stack (language, framework, runtime). Captures the decision context, constraints, and trade-offs of choosing a tech stack.

nodes.label: ADR: {{STACK}}
nodes.context: We need to select a primary technology stack for the new service / module. Key factors: team expertise, ecosystem maturity, performance characteristics, hiring market, and long-term maintainability.
nodes.decision: We will use {{STACK}} because [RATIONALE]. This choice balances team familiarity, ecosystem support, and performance requirements.
nodes.consequences:
• Team members unfamiliar with the stack will need onboarding (estimated 2-3 weeks)
• We gain access to [ECOSYSTEM BENEFIT]
• Lock-in: migrating away would require [EFFORT ESTIMATE]
nodes.alternatives:
1. [Alternative A] — rejected because [reason]
2. [Alternative B] — rejected because [reason]
3. [Alternative C] — viable but less preferred because [reason]

name: Data Store Selection
description: Template ADR for choosing the primary data store (relational, document, key-value, graph, etc.). Considers consistency, scalability, query patterns, and operational complexity.

nodes.label: ADR: {{DATABASE}}
nodes.context: The service needs a persistent data store. Key considerations: data model complexity (relational vs document), consistency requirements (strong vs eventual), expected query patterns (OLTP vs OLAP), scalability needs, and operational team capabilities.
nodes.decision: We will use {{DATABASE}} as the primary data store because [RATIONALE]. It provides the right balance of consistency, query flexibility, and operational simplicity for our use case.
nodes.consequences:
• Schema management via [TOOL/APPROACH]
• Backup and DR strategy: [APPROACH]
• Vendor lock-in risk: [LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH]
• Estimated operational cost: [ESTIMATE]
nodes.alternatives:
1. [Alternative DB A] — rejected: [reason]
2. [Alternative DB B] — rejected: [reason]
3. Multi-model approach — rejected: operational complexity too high at current team size

name: Authentication Strategy
description: Template ADR for choosing the authentication mechanism (JWT, session-based, OAuth 2.0, OIDC, mTLS). Covers security, scalability, and user experience trade-offs.

nodes.label: ADR: {{AUTH_MECHANISM}}
nodes.context: We need to authenticate users and services. Requirements: support for SSO, token-based stateless auth for APIs, refresh token rotation, and compatibility with our frontend framework. Must support both human users and machine-to-machine (M2M) scenarios.
nodes.decision: We will use {{AUTH_MECHANISM}} because [RATIONALE]. Human users authenticate via [FLOW], machine clients via [FLOW].
nodes.consequences:
• Token storage: [APPROACH]
• Token lifetime and refresh strategy: [DETAILS]
• Revocation mechanism: [APPROACH]
• Impact on latency: [ESTIMATE]

name: Integration Pattern
description: Template ADR for choosing between synchronous (REST/gRPC) and asynchronous (event-driven/messaging) integration between services. Key architectural decision for distributed systems.

templateParams.label: Integration pattern
templateParams.hint: REST / gRPC for sync, Kafka for async

nodes.label: ADR: {{PATTERN}}
nodes.context: Services need to communicate. We must decide between synchronous request-response (REST, gRPC) and asynchronous messaging (events, commands via message broker). Factors: latency requirements, coupling tolerance, reliability needs, ordering guarantees, and team familiarity.
nodes.decision: We will use {{PATTERN}} as the default integration style because [RATIONALE]. Exceptions: [LIST EXCEPTIONS AND WHY].
nodes.consequences:
• Debugging and tracing: [APPROACH — correlation IDs, distributed tracing]
• Error handling: [APPROACH — retries, DLQ, circuit breakers]
• Data consistency: [EVENTUAL vs STRONG, saga pattern if needed]
• Infrastructure: [BROKER/GATEWAY needed]
nodes.alternatives:
1. Pure sync (REST) — rejected: creates runtime coupling chains
2. Pure async — rejected: adds complexity for simple queries
3. Hybrid (proposed) — sync for queries, async for commands/events

name: Deployment Strategy
description: Template ADR for choosing the deployment strategy (blue-green, canary, rolling, recreate). Balances risk, rollback speed, and infrastructure cost.

nodes.label: ADR: {{DEPLOY_STRATEGY}} Deployment
nodes.context: We need a reliable deployment strategy that minimizes downtime and risk. Considerations: zero-downtime requirement, rollback speed, infrastructure cost, database migration compatibility, and observability during rollout.
nodes.decision: We will use {{DEPLOY_STRATEGY}} deployments because [RATIONALE]. Rollback is achieved by [MECHANISM].
nodes.consequences:
• Infrastructure cost: [ESTIMATE — e.g. 2x for blue-green]
• Rollback time: [ESTIMATE]
• Database migration approach: [APPROACH — backwards-compatible migrations]
• Monitoring during rollout: [METRICS TO WATCH]
nodes.alternatives:
1. Blue-green — instant rollback but 2x infrastructure cost
2. Canary — gradual rollout, requires good observability
3. Rolling — least infrastructure overhead but slower rollback
4. Recreate — simplest but causes downtime

name: Observability Strategy
description: Template ADR for choosing the observability stack and approach (logs, metrics, traces). Covers the three pillars of observability and their tooling.

nodes.label: ADR: Observability Strategy
nodes.context: We need a comprehensive observability strategy covering the three pillars: structured logging, metrics, and distributed tracing. The strategy must support debugging production issues, measuring SLOs, and understanding system behaviour across service boundaries.
nodes.decision: We will implement OpenTelemetry for instrumentation. Logs: {{LOG_TOOL}}. Metrics: {{METRICS_TOOL}}. Traces: {{TRACES_TOOL}}. Dashboards: [TOOL].
nodes.consequences:
• All services must emit structured logs in JSON format
• Correlation IDs propagated via [HEADER] across all service calls
• SLO dashboards created for each critical user journey
• Estimated additional infrastructure cost: [ESTIMATE]
nodes.alternatives:
1. [Alternative stack A] — rejected: [reason]
2. Vendor-locked solution — rejected: portability concern
3. Build custom — rejected: undifferentiated heavy lifting

name: Microservices Architecture
description: A microservices decomposition with an API Gateway, two backend services, shared database per service, and message queue for async communication. Each service owns its data store.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Entry point for all client requests. Routes, authenticates, and rate-limits incoming traffic.

nodes.label: Service A
nodes.description: Handles domain capability A. Owns its data store and exposes APIs consumed by the gateway.

nodes.label: Service A Database
nodes.description: Private data store for Service A. Not shared with other services.

nodes.label: Service B
nodes.description: Handles domain capability B. Communicates with Service A via events.

nodes.label: Service B Database
nodes.description: Private data store for Service B.

nodes.label: Message Broker
nodes.description: Async event bus for inter-service communication. Decouples services temporally.

name: Event-Driven Architecture
description: An event-driven system where producers emit domain events to a central event bus, and multiple consumers react independently. Enables loose coupling and scalable processing.

nodes.label: Event Producer
nodes.description: Generates domain events when state changes occur. Events are immutable facts about what happened.

nodes.label: Event Bus
nodes.description: Central event backbone. Persists events and delivers them to subscribed consumers. Supports replay and partitioning.

nodes.label: Consumer: Projections
nodes.description: Builds read-optimized views (CQRS query side) by consuming domain events.

nodes.label: Consumer: Notifications
nodes.description: Sends notifications (email, push, SMS) in response to domain events.

name: CQRS + Event Sourcing
description: Separates command (write) and query (read) models. Commands produce events stored in an event store; projections build read-optimized views from those events.

nodes.label: Command Handler
nodes.description: Validates and processes commands. Produces domain events. Enforces business invariants.

nodes.label: Event Store
nodes.description: Append-only log of all domain events. The single source of truth. Supports event replay for rebuilding state.

nodes.label: Projection Engine
nodes.description: Consumes events and builds read-optimized views. Runs asynchronously and can be rebuilt from scratch.

nodes.label: Read Store
nodes.description: Denormalized read model optimized for query patterns. Updated by projections.

nodes.label: Query Handler
nodes.description: Serves read requests from the denormalized read store. No business logic, no writes.

name: API Gateway Pattern
description: Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, routing, TLS termination) in a single entry point. Backend services focus on business logic without repeating infrastructure concerns.

nodes.label: API Consumer
nodes.description: External client (web app, mobile app, third-party integration) that consumes the public API.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Edge proxy handling: TLS termination, authentication, rate limiting, request routing, response caching, and API versioning.

nodes.label: User Service
nodes.description: Manages user accounts, profiles, and preferences.

nodes.label: Order Service
nodes.description: Handles order creation, processing, and fulfilment.

name: Strangler Fig Migration
description: Incrementally migrates a monolith to a new architecture by routing traffic through a facade. New features go to the new system; legacy features are migrated one by one until the monolith can be decommissioned.

nodes.label: Strangler Facade
nodes.description: Routes requests to either the legacy monolith or the new services based on feature flags / URL patterns. Transparent to consumers.

nodes.label: Legacy Monolith
nodes.description: The existing system being migrated. Features are removed from here as they're rebuilt in the new services.

nodes.label: New Service
nodes.description: Modern replacement for a subset of monolith functionality. Built with the target architecture.

nodes.label: New Service Database
nodes.description: Dedicated data store for the new service. Data migrated from the monolith's shared database.

name: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports & Adapters)
description: Isolates business logic from infrastructure via ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations). The domain core has no dependencies on frameworks, databases, or external services.

nodes.label: Domain Core
nodes.description: Pure business logic and domain rules. No infrastructure dependencies. Defines ports (interfaces) that adapters implement.

nodes.label: Application Service
nodes.description: Orchestrates use cases by calling domain objects and port interfaces. Thin layer — no business rules here.

nodes.label: REST Adapter (Driving)
nodes.description: Inbound adapter: translates HTTP requests into application service calls. Implements the driving port.

nodes.label: Database Adapter (Driven)
nodes.description: Outbound adapter: implements the repository port to persist domain objects in a database.

nodes.label: External API Adapter (Driven)
nodes.description: Outbound adapter: implements a port to call external services (payment, email, etc.).

name: Analytical Data Platform
description: A complete blueprint for an analytical data platform (data warehouse / lakehouse). Includes core systems, EARS requirements, fitness functions, and architecture decisions. Import all or select individual elements.

nodes.label: Data Ingestion
nodes.description: Collects raw data from operational systems, third-party APIs, and event streams. Handles batch and streaming loads.

nodes.label: Data Lake / Warehouse
nodes.description: Stores raw, curated, and aggregated data. Supports both schema-on-read (lake) and schema-on-write (warehouse) patterns.

nodes.label: Transformation Layer
nodes.description: Applies business logic, cleansing, and aggregation rules to produce analytical datasets. Supports incremental and full-refresh models.

nodes.label: Serving / BI Layer
nodes.description: Exposes curated datasets to business users via dashboards, embedded analytics, and self-service query tools.

nodes.label: Ingestion Latency
nodes.action: deliver all streaming events to the data lake within {{INGESTION_LATENCY_SECONDS}} seconds of generation
nodes.rationale: Near-real-time data enables operational dashboards and timely alerting.

nodes.label: Data Quality Gate
nodes.action: reject or quarantine any record that fails schema validation or referential-integrity checks before loading into the curated zone
nodes.rationale: Prevents propagation of bad data into analytical models and dashboards.

nodes.label: Role-based Access
nodes.action: enforce column- and row-level security based on the viewer's organisational role, with no exceptions
nodes.rationale: Sensitive financial and personal data must not be accessible to unauthorised roles.

nodes.label: Data Lineage
nodes.action: record full column-level lineage for every transformation and expose it through the catalogue API
nodes.rationale: Lineage is required for regulatory audits and enables trust in analytical outputs.

nodes.label: Data Freshness Guard
nodes.description: Verifies that each critical dataset in the warehouse is refreshed within its SLA window. Alerts on-call if a model has not run within the agreed interval.
nodes.threshold: All critical models refreshed within their SLA (default: {{FRESHNESS_SLA_HOURS}} h)

nodes.label: Data Quality Score
nodes.description: Runs dbt tests or Great Expectations suites after each transformation run. Fails the pipeline and alerts if the pass-rate drops below threshold.

nodes.label: Query Cost Guard
nodes.description: Tracks cloud warehouse credit consumption per team/project. Blocks scheduling of ad-hoc queries that would exceed the daily budget.

templateParams.label: Daily warehouse budget cap per team

nodes.label: ADR-001: Lakehouse over pure DWH
nodes.context: The organisation needs to support both structured reporting (SQL/BI tools) and unstructured / ML workloads on the same data. A traditional relational DWH cannot cost-effectively store raw JSON, image metadata, or large model artefacts.
nodes.decision: Adopt a lakehouse architecture (Delta Lake / Iceberg open table format on object storage) as the primary storage layer, with a semantic layer (Snowflake / BigQuery) for curated, high-performance analytical queries.
nodes.consequences: Teams can run ML experiments directly on raw data without ETL. Storage costs decrease vs a full DWH copy. Governance requires careful management of open-format schemas and compaction jobs.
nodes.alternatives: 1. Pure cloud DWH (Snowflake only) — simpler but expensive at ML scale. 2. Hadoop-based data lake — mature but operationally heavy.

nodes.label: ADR-002: dbt as transformation standard
nodes.context: Multiple teams write transformation logic in ad-hoc SQL scripts, Spark jobs, and stored procedures. Lack of version control, testing, and documentation creates data trust issues.
nodes.decision: Standardise all curated-zone transformations on dbt (data build tool). All models must have at least one schema test and a description. Spark is permitted for raw ingestion and heavy ML feature engineering only.
nodes.consequences: Improved lineage, automated testing, and documentation. Learning curve for teams unfamiliar with dbt. CI/CD pipeline required for model deployment.
nodes.alternatives: 1. Custom Spark jobs — more flexible but no built-in lineage or testing conventions. 2. Stored procedures — tightly coupled to warehouse vendor.
